assumptions.statement: spec/.runtime/*.jsonl logs (land-log.jsonl, spawn-log.jsonl, boot-cite-log.jsonl) are append-only -- no tool ever rewrites or truncates a prior entry.
At most one human dev-resolver session drives waves at a time -- no concurrent multi-resolver editing of the same graph.

conflicts.context: T1 targeted-enforcer gate on every apply_proposal call vs mandatory full T2 pytest suite at wave/commit boundaries -- T2 runs have hit multi-minute timeouts in this repo (observed Wave 2), creating real pressure to skip or shrink T2, which would undermine R-wave-lands-atomically
conflicts.lifecycle: DECIDED(Resolved 2026-07-05 both ways per R-conflict-resolved-in-members-or-mediator: members amended (test suite sped 223s->~55s, gen_spec 20s->3s) AND a mediating rule created (R-run-speed-guarded self-calibrating guard) so the speed need cannot silently regress. Resolver verdict.)

conflicts.context: R-wave-strictly-sequential demands that waves touching overlapping files/scopes run strictly sequentially (never concurrently) to avoid racing a shared working tree, while R-worktree-parallel-permitted sanctions running mutating pipeline agents in parallel when each is isolated in its own git worktree -- the same pipeline both forbids overlapping-scope concurrency and permits isolated concurrency, and the boundary (when is isolation sufficient to relax strict sequencing?) is undecided
conflicts.lifecycle: DECIDED(no real tension: worktree isolation removes the shared-files premise; the sequentiality rule governs shared working copies only. Resolver verdict 2026-07-03 (V5), verbatim: «здесь нет противоерчия. Worktree - это как бы не параллельно». A worktree is not truly parallel work on the same files -- each worktree is an isolated copy, so the strict-sequential wave rule (which exists to prevent two agents clobbering one shared working tree) simply does not apply there; the two requirements never actually collide. Decided by dev-resolver, 2026-07-03.)

note: READ-ONLY generated snapshot. Source of truth is the domain's graph.json; edit the graph only via `hotam apply-proposal` (R-no-hand-edit-graph, R-per-node-json-store REJECTED).

requirements.why: Reworded 2026-07-12 to describe the current implementation. internal/gate.SelectTier1 (internal/gate/gate.go) resolves a target anchor's enforced_by into the union of concrete check_*/Test* enforcers, exposed as the STANDALONE advisory command `hotam gate <target>` (cmd/hotam/gate_cmd.go) -- it is NOT wired into `hotam land` or `hotam apply-proposal`, both of which run the full all-violations pass instead. (Previously the mechanism ran T1 by default on every apply as `tools/apply_proposal.py` consulting `tools/gate.py`.) The intent that T1 should become the land default is held open as DRAFT R-t1-selector-as-land-default (hotam-spec-self). The original WHY stands: per-move full-suite verification (go test ./...) is too slow for the conveyor's normal cadence; T1 keeps the loop responsive while go test ./... remains the mandatory boundary gate (R-wave-lands-atomically). STRUCTURAL (not ENFORCED): the wiring that would make this TRUE-by-default is an open DRAFT, not a realized behavior.

requirements.why: Reworded 2026-07-12 to describe the current implementation. T1 alone (see R-t1-gate-is-default) only checks the targeted enforcer subset per move; without a mandatory full-suite gate at the wave boundary, cross-cutting regressions between moves in the same wave could slip through undetected. `hotam land --batch` (cmd/hotam/land.go -> proposal.ApplyBatch) lands a whole directory of proposals atomically (all-or-nothing) with one apply -> gen-spec -> all-violations pass, and spec/CLAUDE.md's Mediation loop step 6 LAND names `go test ./...` as MANDATORY at wave/commit boundaries. Enforcement is honestly STRUCTURAL: what-now/land make the T2 boundary visible and addressable but no single check_* fires automatically if a resolver skips a wave-boundary `go test ./...` by hand outside the tool (the gate_status / land-log.jsonl tier trace that would have made this auditable is not yet implemented -- see R-land-leaves-trace).

The category distinction still holds: an explicit spawn_agent invocation composing a domain sub-operator's crystal -- currently ZERO such sub-operators in this domain, per the Agent Map '(no sub-operators yet)' -- is category-distinct from a host-level Agent-tool spawn, covered by R-host-spawn-leaves-trace. The original WHY stands: without a trace of every spawn, R-delegation-conclusions-only and R-task-spawn-log-runtime (hotam-spec-self) cannot be audited after the fact. STRUCTURAL (not ENFORCED): no enforcer exists; this atom stands as the discipline that the trace SHOULD exist for every spawn.

(Previously implemented as a `spec/.runtime/land-log.jsonl` writer recording the verification tier T1/T2 and the test outcome, which gate_status read.) The original WHY stands: without an auditable land-log trace there is no durable record of what actually got verified before landing. PROSE (not ENFORCED): no enforcer exists.

requirements.claim: A commit shall land only after review of the diff by a human or an agent code-review step.
requirements.why: Review is the last human-in-the-loop check before a change becomes permanent history; this is a judgment call about diff quality and intent that no mechanical check can substitute for, so enforcement stays honestly PROSE.

requirements.claim: Push to remote shall occur only on the dev-resolver's explicit request, never autonomously.
requirements.why: A push is externally visible and hard to undo cleanly (shared history); reserving it strictly to explicit resolver request keeps the irreversible action under human authority, mirroring the project's global CLAUDE.md discipline. Not mechanically checkable from inside this repo (no push actually happens in tests), so PROSE.

requirements.claim: Waves touching overlapping files or scopes shall run strictly sequentially, never concurrently.
requirements.why: Concurrent edits to the same files/scope would race and corrupt state (the graph, the docs, the .runtime logs); git history in this repo is evidence of sequential waves but is not itself a cheap mechanical gate, so enforcement stays honestly STRUCTURAL/PROSE rather than a fabricated git-log parser.

requirements.why: Not yet implemented: no `.runtime/spawn-log.jsonl` writer exists in this system today (the `.runtime` directory does not exist in the repo). spawn_agent and spawn_log_isolation_status are both Planned in methodology.Tools (not yet available as `hotam` CLI commands), so the host-spawn trace path they would provide is not present. (Previously implemented as `tools/spawn_agent.py --log-only` -- Wave 10 move 2a -- which appended a spawn-log row agent/task-first-line/stamp/isolation/mutating WITHOUT composing a crystal, proven by `test_log_only_writes_row_without_composing_prompt`.) The category distinction from R-task-spawn-log-runtime (hotam-spec-self) still holds: a host-level Agent-tool spawn is a platform primitive external to the framework's own code, distinct from an explicit spawn_agent invocation composing a domain sub-operator's crystal. The original WHY stands: every host spawn must leave a durable runtime trace so the isolation discipline is auditable. STRUCTURAL (not ENFORCED): no enforcer exists; whether the operator actually records each host spawn is operator discipline, not mechanically provable from the graph.

requirements.claim: Mutating pipeline agents may run in parallel when each is isolated in its own git worktree, so their edits to tracked files cannot collide.
requirements.why: This atom reflects, inside the hotam-dev domain, the self-practice crystallized by the framework atom R-parallel-mutating-agents-use-worktree (hotam-spec-self): after the 2026-06-30/07-01 incident where two parallel agents in one shared tree lost work to a history-rewrite, the pipeline adopted per-agent worktree isolation (isolation:'worktree') as the sanctioned way to run mutating agents concurrently. It is honestly modeled here as a hotam-dev requirement (not a cross-domain reference) because a Conflict's members must be co-domain: it lets the sequential-vs-isolated-parallel tension between overlapping-scope waves (R-wave-strictly-sequential) and isolated parallelism be modeled as a first-class Conflict node in the domain that actually runs the pipeline. Enforcement is PROSE: whether an agent ACTUALLY runs in a worktree is operator discipline, not mechanically provable from this graph.

stakeholders.domain: reviews and approves waves/commits; sole authority to request a push
stakeholders.name: Dev resolver

stakeholders.domain: executes waves, applies proposals, spawns sub-agents
stakeholders.name: Pipeline operator
